Notice Of Hearing And Motion To Vacate Judgment
Off Calendar Oct-22-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances via Zoom Conference:
Kamlesh Banga self-represented plaintiff.
DAVID T. SHUEY, ESQ., of RANKIN, SHUEY, MINTZ, LAMPASONA & HARPER., Tel:510-433-2600 for defendants.

Hearing held. A untimely Notice of Contest was received from self-represented plaintiff. The matter was discussed. Having reviewed the pleadings, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

1 - Plaintiff's Kamlesh Banga's Motion to Vacate Judgment is DENIED.

Plaintiff relies on Code of Civil Procedure section 473(d) as the basis for her motion. Section 473(d) provides: "[t]he court may, . . . on motion of either party after notice to the other party, set aside any void judgment or order." Because Plaintiff had a full and fair opportunity to litigate the underlying issues during the original proceedings, equitable relief under section 473(d) is not warranted.

The crux of Plaintiff's motion is the assertion that Defendant misrepresented the factual allegations underlying the so-called "spoliation claim," and that the trial court's ruling sustaining the demurrer was therefore based on fraudulent statements. However, Plaintiff overlooks the fact that she had - and exercised - multiple opportunities to litigate the issue of Defendant's alleged failure to provide complete copies of her medical records. She pursued that issue over the course of several years and had a full and fair chance to present evidence, raise objections, and argue her position. As the court in People v. Egan (1946) 73 Cal.App.2d 894, 899, explained:

Equitable relief will be denied where it is sought to relitigate an issue involved in the former proceeding on the ground that allegations or proof of either party was fraudulent or based on mistake, but such relief may be granted if the party seeking it was precluded by fraud or the mistake of the other party from participating in the proceeding or from fully presenting his case.

Here, Plaintiff was not precluded from participating in the proceedings or from presenting her claim regarding the alleged withholding of records. Plaintiff actively litigated her tort claims for damages regarding production of her medical records (which were barred as prohibited tort claims for spoliation) and voluntarily dismissed her equitable claims under the Health and Safety and Evidence codes (seeking fees and costs). The court will not set aside the judgment now (these many years later) because Plaintiff now characterizes the dispute differently or alleges misrepresentation. Plaintiff had a full opportunity to litigate the issues. Thus, her motion to vacate the judgment is without basis and is denied.

Order signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

Hearing held. A untimely Notice of Contest was received from self-represented plaintiff. The matter was discussed. Having reviewed the pleadings, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

2 - Plaintiff Kamlesh Banga's unopposed Motion to File an Oversized Memorandum is GRANTED.

Under California Rules of Court, rule 3.1113(d), no memorandum may exceed 15 pages without prior permission from the court. Plaintiff has filed a motion accompanied by a 35-page memorandum but failed to comply with the proper procedures for requesting an oversized memorandum. Specifically, rule 3.1113(e) requires a party to seek permission by way of an ex parte application before filing an oversized memorandum. Plaintiff did not submit such a request. Litigants are expected to follow standard court procedures to ensure the efficient use of judicial resources. While Plaintiff did not obtain the required permission to exceed the page limit, the court will, in this instance, allow the oversized memorandum.

Order is signed.

Off Calendar Mar-04-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. TINA L. LI, Esq. of Rankin, Shuey, Ranucci, Mintz, Lampasona & Reynolds (Tel: 510-433-2600) for defendants;
No other appearances made in this matter.

Hearing Re: Plaintiff Kamlesh Banga's Motion To Stay Proceedings Pending Appeal.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to stay all proceedings pending appeal is denied. By order filed October 6, 2020, the Court sustained Defendants' demurrer without leave to amend as to certain causes of action in Plaintiff's third amended complaint, and on December 17, 2020, it denied Plaintiff's motion for reconsideration of that order and of Plaintiff's motion for leave to file a fourth amended complaint. Although Plaintiff has purported to appeal from the latter orders, they are not appealable orders, as Plaintiff has causes of action remaining for resolution in the case. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 904.1; see In re Baycol Cases I & II (2011) 51 Cal.4th 751, 752 ["The right to appeal in California is generally governed by the 'one final judgment' rule, under which most interlocutory orders are not appealable"]; Hill v. City of Clovis (1998) 63 Cal.App.4th 434, 442-433 ["An appeal cannot be taken from a judgment which fails to complete the disposition of all the causes of action pending between the parties."].) Accordingly, there is no basis for the requested stay of proceedings.

Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the Court's ruling.

Notice Of Motion For Leave Include Additional Claims In The Third Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-17-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom Conference:
Kamlesh Banga, kkbanga@gmail.com, 707-342-1692 for Pro Per Plaintiff.
Tina Li, Esq. li@rankinlaw.com, Rankin Sjuey Ranucci, Mintz, et al 510-433-2600 for Defendants the Regents of the University of California et al

ARGUED.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument, adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF KAMLESH BANGA Notice Of Motion For Leave Include Additional Claims In The Third Amended Complaint

Plaintiff's motion for leave to file a Fourth Amended Complaint is denied. As an initial matter, Plaintiff's motion is procedurally defective, as it is not supported by a declaration that conforms to California Rule of Court 3.1324(b). In particular, Plaintiff fails to supply a declaration that explains "[w]hen the facts giving rise to the amended allegations were discovered" and "[t]he reasons why the request for amendment was not made earlier." (Cal. R. Ct. 3.1324(b)(3),(4).) In addition, "even if a good amendment is proposed in proper form, unwarranted delay in presenting it may-of itself-be a valid reason for denial." (Huff v. Wilkins (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 732, 746, quoting Record v. Reason (1999) 73 Cal.App.4th 472, 486.) Here, as set forth in the separate ruling denying Plaintiff's motion for reconsideration, the documents and facts giving rise to the proposed amendment have been known to Plaintiff since 2009, 2012 and 2013, and this action has been pending since January 2016. Further, Plaintiff seeks leave to add claims that this Court has already conclusively ruled upon, that violate the Court's prior orders, that are inconsistent with her prior verified complaints, and that are time-barred or otherwise defective on their face. "[U]nder the sham pleading doctrine, the trial court may disregard amendments that omit harmful allegations in the original complaint or add allegations inconsistent with it." (Falcon v. Long Beach Genetics, Inc. (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 1263, 1281.) Under the circumstances, the motion must be denied.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. (302/EPS)

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF KAMLESH BANGA'S Motion For Leave To Include Additional Claims In The Third Amended Complaint

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Kamlesh Banga's motion for leave to include additional claims in the third amended complaint is granted. The court understands that plaintiff has already included the proposed additional claims in the third amended complaint, which she filed without leave of court, and defendants have filed a demurrer and motion to strike in response to that pleading, which are set for hearing on October 6, 2020. If, as defendants contend, the new claims fail to state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action, the court will decide those issues in connection with defendants' motions. (See California Casualty Gen. Ins. Co. v. Superior Court (1985) 173 Cal.App.3d 274, 281 ["the preferable practice would be to permit the amendment and allow the parties to test its legal sufficiency by demurrer, motion for judgment on the pleadings or other appropriate proceedings."].) The Court admonishes plaintiff that although she is representing herself in propria persona, she must comply with procedural rules, including seeking prior leave of court to file any amended pleading and compliance with California Rule of Court 3.1324. (See ViaView, Inc. v. Retzlaff (2016) 1 Cal.App.5th 198, 208 ["While a party may choose to act as his or her own attorney, such a party is to be treated like any other party and is entitled to the same, but no greater consideration than other litigants and attorneys. Thus, as is the case with attorneys, self-represented litigants must follow correct rules of procedure."].)

Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare and submit a proposed order.
